conditions.name: baseline
conditions.rationale: Canonical Phase 3.3 world (density 60, unposted 0.15, no competition, fuel 0.55x1.0, HOS 11h, hub-sampled equipment, 7-day horizon). Reconciles with the shipped rolling-replay result.

conditions.name: density_low
conditions.rationale: Thin board (25 loads/snapshot). Fewer onward options near every destination — does the destination signal still find the good exits when supply is scarce, or does it just chase a near-empty market?

conditions.name: density_high
conditions.rationale: Flooded board (120 loads/snapshot). With options everywhere, deadhead is easy to avoid for either policy — the destination edge may compress.

conditions.name: unposted_035
conditions.rationale: 35% of postings are "call for rate" (non-viable). Shrinks the priced board and the onward supply the model reasons over.

conditions.name: unposted_050
conditions.rationale: Half the board is unpriced. A stress on label sparsity: the learned onward-deadhead signal must survive a much thinner priced market.

conditions.name: competition_025
conditions.rationale: A quarter of visible loads are taken first by rival demand, biased toward high-view (contested) loads. Both policies face the same thinned board — does destination-awareness help when the best loads vanish?

conditions.name: competition_050
conditions.rationale: Half the visible board is skimmed by rivals (view-biased). The strongest single test of whether the onward signal survives a hostile market.

conditions.name: fuel_095
conditions.rationale: Fuel jumps 0.55 -> 0.95 $/mi (rebuilds the cost stack end-to-end). Every empty mile hurts more, so reducing onward deadhead should matter more.

conditions.name: deadhead_15
conditions.rationale: Empty miles burn 1.5x fuel (loaded miles unchanged). Directly amplifies the quantity the destination model is built to reduce.

conditions.name: hos_strict_8
conditions.rationale: Strict 8h daily drive cap. Less reach per day, so a bad destination is costlier to recover from — sequential positioning should matter more.

conditions.name: hos_relaxed_14
conditions.rationale: Relaxed 14h cap. The truck can drive out of a bad spot more easily, which may wash out the destination advantage.

conditions.name: equip_hs
conditions.rationale: Pin every truck to hot-shot (HS/Reefer). Tests the signal on a single equipment market instead of the hub-weighted mix.

conditions.name: equip_f
conditions.rationale: Pin every truck to flatbed (F). A different supply geography — does the destination edge hold for a thinner, more specialised trailer class?

conditions.name: horizon_3
conditions.rationale: Short 3-day horizon. Fewer sequential decisions to compound the signal — expect a smaller but ideally still-positive effect.

conditions.name: horizon_14
conditions.rationale: Long 14-day horizon. More decisions to compound — the destination advantage should, if real, grow with sequence length.

conditions.name: corner_thin_market
conditions.rationale: Thin + unpriced + contested all at once: scarce supply, half unpriced, half the rest skimmed by rivals. Can the signal still position well when good exits are genuinely rare?

conditions.name: corner_expensive_miles
conditions.rationale: Expensive empty miles under a tight HOS cap. The economics most favour cutting onward deadhead — the destination policy should look best here.

conditions.name: corner_worst_case
conditions.rationale: Everything hostile: scarce, contested, expensive, HOS-constrained, over a long horizon. The decisive robustness test — if the edge survives here it is not a one-world artifact.
